title: Neurocysticercosis: Taenia solium scolex
vignette: A 33-year-old man is admitted to the hospital due to new-onset seizures. He has no significant medical history. Vital signs are normal. Neurologic examination shows no abnormalities. CT scan of the head reveals a 3-cm cystic lesion in the temporal lobe. The mass is excised, and histopathology shows an invaginated scolex with refractile hooklets and suckers (tapeworm larva). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

clues.note: New-onset seizures in an adult from a cystic brain lesion is the classic presentation of neurocysticercosis — the most common cause of acquired epilepsy in endemic regions. Seizures arise as cysts degenerate and inflame surrounding cortex.

clues.text: cystic lesion in the temporal lobe
clues.note: A solitary/multiple brain cyst (often with a scolex inside on imaging) is typical of cysticercosis. Cysts can calcify over time; degenerating cysts trigger the seizures.

clues.text: invaginated scolex with refractile hooklets and suckers
clues.note: A scolex bearing HOOKLETS and SUCKERS is the larval form (cysticercus) of the tapeworm Taenia solium. Hooklets + suckers attach the worm to host tissue — this histology is diagnostic of cysticercosis.

whatMatters: A brain cyst causing new seizures, with biopsy showing a tapeworm scolex (refractile hooklets + suckers), is neurocysticercosis — the larval (cysticercus) form of Taenia solium. Answer D.
strategy: Match the histologic fingerprint: a cyst containing a SCOLEX with hooklets/suckers = Taenia solium cysticercus (neurocysticercosis). Acquired via fecal-oral ingestion of T. solium EGGS (not undercooked pork, which gives intestinal tapeworm). Seizures + brain cysts in someone from an endemic area = neurocysticercosis.

eliminations.label: Echinococcus — hydatid cyst, different histology
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: Echinococcus granulosus causes hydatid cysts (usually liver/lung) with daughter cysts and brood capsules; eggshell-like calcification. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it doesn't typically present as an isolated temporal-lobe cyst with a single invaginated scolex with hooklets/suckers; cyst rupture risks anaphylaxis. ✅ Correct if: imaging showed a large liver cyst with daughter cysts and a history of dogs/sheep.

eliminations.label: Entamoeba histolytica — liver abscess, not brain cyst
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: E. histolytica causes amebic dysentery and 'anchovy-paste' liver abscesses. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's a protozoan, not a tapeworm; no scolex/hooklets, and brain involvement is rare and abscess-like, not a cyst with a worm larva. ✅ Correct if: the lesion were a right-lobe liver abscess after bloody diarrhea.

eliminations.label: Naegleria fowleri — fulminant meningoencephalitis, not a cyst
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: Naegleria (freshwater amoeba, via the cribriform plate) causes rapidly fatal primary amebic meningoencephalitis. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it produces acute, lethal encephalitis with motile trophozoites in CSF — not a chronic temporal-lobe cyst with a tapeworm scolex. ✅ Correct if: a young swimmer had rapidly progressive fatal meningoencephalitis.

eliminations.label: Toxoplasma gondii — ring-enhancing lesions, no scolex
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: reactivated toxoplasmosis (in AIDS) causes multiple RING-ENHANCING brain lesions with tachyzoites/bradyzoite cysts. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this immunocompetent patient has a single cyst containing a SCOLEX with hooklets — that's a helminth (Taenia), not a protozoan; Toxo shows no scolex. ✅ Correct if: an HIV/AIDS patient had multiple ring-enhancing lesions and positive Toxo serology.

reasoning: New-onset seizures + brain cyst in an adult
Histology shows a scolex with refractile hooklets and suckers (tapeworm larva)
Larval cysticercus in the CNS = neurocysticercosis
Causative organism = Taenia solium → answer D
mechanism: Neurocysticercosis is CNS infection by the larval cysticercus of the pork tapeworm Taenia solium, acquired by fecal-oral ingestion of EGGS (autoinfection or contaminated food/water). Larvae encyst in the brain; as cysts degenerate they incite inflammation and edema, causing seizures (the leading cause of acquired epilepsy in endemic areas), and may calcify. Histology/imaging shows an invaginated scolex bearing hooklets and suckers. (Eating undercooked pork with cysticerci instead causes intestinal taeniasis, not neurocysticercosis.)

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Micro > Cestodes: Taenia solium — neurocysticercosis (eggs, fecal-oral) vs intestinal taeniasis (undercooked pork/cysticerci), scolex with hooklets/suckers, seizures + brain cysts, albendazole ± steroids. Contrast Echinococcus, Toxoplasma, free-living amoebae.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Anchor on the histology: a scolex with hooklets/suckers is a TAPEWORM larva. Cyst + seizures + scolex = neurocysticercosis (T. solium).

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on scolex, hooklets, cysticercus, cestode, neurocysticercosis, ring-enhancing lesion? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Toxoplasma (E) is the reflex 'brain lesion' answer, but it's a protozoan with ring-enhancing lesions and NO scolex; the worm parts make it Taenia. Read the histology, not just 'brain mass.'

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The slide shows an invaginated scolex with refractile hooklets and two suckers. Learn to spot cestode larval anatomy (hooklets + suckers) vs protozoal cysts/trophozoites and vascular tumors.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: The answer choices were scrolled off-screen in your screenshot (only the stem + histology image were visible), so the option wording here is reconstructed from standard content — the diagnosis (neurocysticercosis, Taenia solium) is certain. Verify the exact choices against UWorld if you want them word-perfect.

keywords.front: Brain cyst + new seizures + scolex with hooklets/suckers

keywords.back: Fecal-oral ingestion of T. solium EGGS

keywords.front: Leading cause of acquired epilepsy (endemic areas)

keywords.back: Hydatid liver cysts (daughter cysts); anaphylaxis on rupture

keywords.front: Multiple ring-enhancing brain lesions in AIDS

firstAid.detail: Micro > Cestodes: egg ingestion → cysticercosis (brain cysts, seizures) vs pork cysticerci → intestinal taeniasis; scolex morphology; treatment.
firstAid.location: Micro > Cestodes: egg ingestion → cysticercosis (brain cysts, seizures) vs pork cysticerci → intestinal taeniasis; scolex morphology; treatment.

firstAid.detail: Micro: Toxoplasma (ring-enhancing, AIDS), Naegleria (PAM), Echinococcus (hydatid), Entamoeba (liver abscess).
firstAid.location: Micro: Toxoplasma (ring-enhancing, AIDS), Naegleria (PAM), Echinococcus (hydatid), Entamoeba (liver abscess).

firstAid.detail: Neuro/Micro: cystic brain lesions, calcified granulomas, acquired epilepsy etiologies.
firstAid.location: Neuro/Micro: cystic brain lesions, calcified granulomas, acquired epilepsy etiologies.

memoryHook: Scolex with hooklets + suckers in a brain cyst + seizures = the pork tapeworm Taenia solium (neurocysticercosis from EGGS).
